coredata.dc:title: Protective layer β-cyclodextrin within peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells’ nanoparticles enhances intracellular stable fluorescence for bioimaging applications: An in vitro and in silico study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ilhami, Fasih Bintang
Puspitarini, Sapti
Fitriana,
Rahmawati, Astrid
Mayasari, Noor Rohmah
Herliniati, Hepy
coredata.dc:description: Owing to their excellent fluorescence behavior in the past decades, carbon nanoparticles have gained remarkable attention for a wide range of biomedical applications. However, the synthesis and solubility of carbon nanoparticles in an aqueous solution remains a challenging area of study. In this work, we synthesized carbon nanoparticles from peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells with a protected layer of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as a highly stable fluorescent material for bioimaging applications. These nanoparticles possess highly stable photoluminescence and optical absorption properties in an aqueous solution. Additionally, in vitro kinetic stability confirmed that β-CD-coated carbon nanoparticles are stable under serum-rich environments and exhibit antihemolytic properties. Predominantly, in vitro cytotoxicity studies clearly demonstrated that β-CD-coated carbon nanoparticles possess cytotoxicity, rapid intracellular uptake, and stable fluorescence under HeLa cells. Moreover, an in-silico study confirmed that β-CD-coated carbon nanoparticles could specifically interact with the target protein of CDK-2, which plays a role in inhibiting cancer cell growth. Hence, this new study has shown that in tracking cancer cells, high-value-added peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells with a β-CD protective layer are effective in improving solubility and intracellular fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peanut shell
β-cyclodextrin
Nanoparticles
Stable fluorescence
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS analysis of CPNs (b) XRD pattern of CPNs, β-CD, and β-CD-coated CPNs.

Optical absorption and fluorescence of CPNs and β-CD-coated CPNs at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL (a) UV-Vis, (b) PL, photographs of carbon nanoparticle and β-CD-coated CPNs under (c) natural (normal) light and (d) broadband of long-wavelength lamp illumination.
DLS results of (a) CPNs (b) β-CD-coated CPNs at concentration 0.1 mg/mL. SEM image with high resolution (c) CPNs, (d) β-CD-coated CPNs.

Time-dependent fluorescence intensity changes behavior of (a) CPNs (b) β-CD-coated CPNs at concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. (c) Mean hydrodynamic diameters of CPNs and β-CD-coated CPNs in DMEM containing 10 % FBS recorded by DLS at 25 °C over time. (d) MRBCs hemolysis assays after incubation with varying concentrations of carbon nanoparticles and β-CD-coated CPNs (0.1–200 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 4 h.

(a) MTT assay of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs under HeLa cells (b) CLSM fluorescence images of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs. Fluorescence histogram profiles derived from flow cytometry analysis towards HeLa cells of (c) CNPs (d) β-CD-coated CNPs.
The visualization of molecular docking between CDK-2 with (a) β-CD-coated CPNs, (b) AZD5438. 2D imaging of interaction between CDK-2 with (c) β-CD-coated CPNs, (d) AZD5438.
Graphical illustration for β-CD-coated carbon nanoparticle for bioimaging.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: “Nanoparticles and polymer-based nanoparticles in plants and microbes” published at the journal Plant Nano Biology.

head.ce:title: Protective layer β-cyclodextrin within peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells’ nanoparticles enhances intracellular stable fluorescence for bioimaging applications: An in vitro and in silico study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their excellent fluorescence behavior in the past decades, carbon nanoparticles have gained remarkable attention for a wide range of biomedical applications. However, the synthesis and solubility of carbon nanoparticles in an aqueous solution remains a challenging area of study. In this work, we synthesized carbon nanoparticles from peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells with a protected layer of β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as a highly stable fluorescent material for bioimaging applications. These nanoparticles possess highly stable photoluminescence and optical absorption properties in an aqueous solution. Additionally, in vitro kinetic stability confirmed that β-CD-coated carbon nanoparticles are stable under serum-rich environments and exhibit antihemolytic properties. Predominantly, in vitro cytotoxicity studies clearly demonstrated that β-CD-coated carbon nanoparticles possess cytotoxicity, rapid intracellular uptake, and stable fluorescence under HeLa cells. Moreover, an in-silico study confirmed that β-CD-coated carbon nanoparticles could specifically interact with the target protein of CDK-2, which plays a role in inhibiting cancer cell growth. Hence, this new study has shown that in tracking cancer cells, high-value-added peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells with a β-CD protective layer are effective in improving solubility and intracellular fluorescence.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticle derivate peanut shell protective layered by β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) intracellular stable fluorescence for bioimaging applications

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanoparticles (CNPs), including graphene nanosheets, nanofibers, fullerenes, nanocrystals, and porous carbon compounds all possess enormous promise for various applications in nanoelectronics (Ayanda et al., 2024), microelectronic devices (Kongsriprapan et al., 2023), electrochemistry (Bettini et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2023), sensors (Kim et al., 2023), catalysis (Chen et al., 2022), bioimaging (Qasim et al., 2023; Pete et al., 2023), and drug delivery (Ma et al., 2023). Recently, CNPs have gained remarkable importance because of their exceptional photoluminescent properties in bioimaging for cancer cells, which is possible because of their ease of extravasation within the circulatory system of cancer cells without entering into normal cells facilitated by their enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR). (Kumara et al., 2023; Algar et al., 2021; Ai et al., 2021). Additionally, these CNPs are biocompatible and chemically inert, which offer advantages over traditional cadmium-based quantum dots (Chatterjee et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2022). Although CNPs produce green or blue light emission, their fluorescent intensity remains lower than that of carbon quantum dots, and their toxicity, attributable to the inadequate solubility in an aqueous solution, is limited to applications especially those under biological or cell imaging (Olabi et al., 2021; Madannejad et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2020; Truskewycz et al., 2022).

Cyclodextrins (CDs) are promising biodegradable substances that are derived from oligosaccharides with a lipophilic central core with hydrophilic outer surfaces, which can be utilized for drug delivery systems due of their advantageous toxicological properties, high water solubility, and natural capacity to partially or fully encapsulate bioactive molecules (e.g., drugs) (Irie and Uekama, 1997). The capacity of CDs molecules to create inclusion complexes is often preserved or even improved when they aggregate, crosslink, or copolymerize with other compounds (Ilhami et al., 2023; Liu and Liu, 2022; Hu et al., 2022). This significantly optimizes the solubility of active pharmaceutical molecules in the physiological environment, thus facilitating the rapid dissolution of solid materials-protected CDs and improving bioavailability (Frijlink et al., 1989). To enhance the existing approaches, the development of a protective layer of 2D material using CDs could significantly optimize their stability in biological environments.

To synthesize CNPs such as arc discharge, laser-induced pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation, thermal decomposition, combustion of natural gas burners, and low-temperature solutions, several methods have been investigated (Hou et al., 2022; Tiwari et al., 2022; Khorshidi et al., 2022). Notably, there remains a challenge in the synthesis of environmentally friendly CNPs that demonstrate high stability in aqueous solutions and have robust photoluminescence properties. In recent years, there has been remarkable interest in green synthetic approaches, which are inexpensive, sustainable and environmentally benign biomass materials as carbon sources to produce materials with one simple method. For instance, Zaini and co-workers (Zaini et al., 2023) synthesized CNPs utilizing watermelon peels as precursors via a carbonization method, demonstrating the colloidal stability and photoluminescence emission under various solvents. Predominantly, Atchudan and co-workers (Atchudan et al., 2021) provided CNPs from banana peels via a simple hydrothermal process, achieving multicolor emissions by varying the excitation wavelength for in vivo bioimaging applications. Thus, the green synthesis of CNPs offers the potential for low-cost, environmentally friendly products that are suitable for a range of biomedical applications.

Peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.), is an annual allotetraploid legume (2 n = 4x = 40) that serves as an important source of vegetable protein and oil (Stalker, 1997). In 2017, Indonesia produced 480,000 tons of dry peanuts, cultivated across 364,000 ha, and yielded 13.18 quintals per hectare. This substantial production generates a remarkable amount of peanut shells (Li et al., 2022), which are among prevalent agricultural byproducts in countries including China, Vietnam, and Indonesia that produce a lot of peanuts (Kim and Dale, 2004). Worldwide, mostly about 7.44 million tons of peanut shells are produced a year, with volatile content reaching up to 60 % (Duan et al., 2014). Despite their abundance, peanut shells are often underutilized and largely discarded, a practice that contradicts sustainability and waste recycling principles. Recently, several studies have emphasized the potential for developing peanut shells as a high-value-added bio-waste for there are several uses for activated carbon, such as in live cell imaging, salt-induced soil improvement, and dye adsorption. (Bhaduri et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2017; Xue et al., 2016). Thus, developing alternative high-value-added bio-waste plant-based precursors, such as peanut shells with protective layered CDs, to enhance stability in an aqueous solution for potential bioimaging application via green synthesis approach gains a strong research interest.

The peanut shells were obtained from local farms in Madura Island, East Java, Indonesia. β-CDs (> 99 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, USA). All chemicals were of analytical grade and utilized without any purification. The ultrapure water was purified using the Milli-Q Plus 185 system.

The reagents and materials required for cellular study. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), trypsin-EDTA, penicillin–streptomycin, methylthiazoltetrazolium (MTT), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA) without any purification. Mouse Red Blood Cells (MRBCs) that were used for the hemolytic test were provided by the Animal Center of Pusvetma Surabaya. The human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells were supplied by the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Rockville, MD, USA) and cultured under the supplier instructions.

Preparation of CNPs. The peanut shells were collected, cleaned, cut into small pieces, and used as material to produce CNPs. The peanut shells were put in a fired-ceramic pot through a pyrolysis process at 250 °C for 2 h at a temperature of 10 °C/min. The obtained peanut shells with a deep black hue color were reduced to a fine powder after the temperature had cooled to ambient temperature. Subsequently, 0.5 g of the sample was dissolved into 50 mL of distilled water using ultrasonication. Finally, CNPs derived from the peanut shells were obtained by removing the bigger particle by passing a filtration membrane (0.22 μm) and kept at 4 °C for further examination.

Synthesis of β-CD-coated CNPs. First, the CNPs (20 mg) dissolved in 1 mL water, which was followed by sonication, and β-CD (75 mg) was dissolved in 1 mL water. Then, the solution of CNPs was immersed into β-CD solution and stirred for 1 h at ambient temperature. The mixture was centrifuged (9500 rpm, 5 min), and the precipitated product was washed on several times with water, then the obtained product was dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS was acquired from ESCALAB 250 XPS equipped with an Al Kα sources (British VG Scientific, Sussex, UK) and employed to validate the formation of the complex and elemental composition of CNPs. The fine powder CNPs were prepared at approximately 25 mg and subsequently put into the holder with leveled. Then, the samples were scanned using XPS instruments.

X-ray diffraction (XRD). XRD pattern was acquired using a Miniflex-600 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) at a scan range of 10°–80° equipped by Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5419 Å) at 30 kV and 10-mA current. First, the fine-powdered samples were prepared at approximately 5 mg and subsequently put into the holder with leveled. Then, the samples were scanned using XRD instruments.

Photoluminescence (PL) and Ultraviolet-Visible (UV-Vis). The fluorescence intensity of samples was recorded using a Hitachi F4500 luminescence spectrometer and Jasco FP-8300 Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and using a Xenon lamp with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm at 25°C. A total of 3 mg of samples were dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water. To ensure complete dissolution subsequently, the resulting mixtures underwent sonication for 5 min; the samples were analyzed using a PL and UV spectrometer.

Particle size. The hydrodynamic particle size and charge stability of samples were determined with a dynamic light scattering particle analyzer (DLS, Nano Brook 90Plus PALS, Brookhaven, USA) apparatus equipped with a 632-nm He–Ne laser beam (scattering angle: 90°). Briefly, 3 mg of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs were dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water. The mixed samples were treated by sonication for 5 min to achieve complete dissolution and then evaluated using a DLS analyzer. Each sample was measured in triplicates with the average value.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The morphology and size of samples were analyzed via SEM (JSM-6500F system JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The samples were diluted in methanol and subsequently vacuum-dried overnight.
Using an excitation source of 350 nm at 25°C for 24 h, the fluorescence intensity of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNP were assessed on a luminescence spectrometer.

The kinetic stability of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNP was examined in DMEM with the presence of 10 % FBS as a destabilizing agent (Ebrahim Attia et al., 2013). The samples were mixed with DMEM consisting of serum solution in a 2:1 v/v. DLS was used to determine the dispersed of light intensity for 24 h period.

MRBCs were utilized to evaluate the hemolytic analysis of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNP as mentioned in a previous study (Liu et al., 2017). Concisely, 1 mL MRBCs and 0.5 mL PBS mixed into a microcentrifuge tube. The mixtures were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant fluid was eliminated as plasma blood. Then, 1.5 mL of PBS was added and shuffled with a Vortex mixer at least 3-times until a clear supernatant was observed. Subsequently, 500-µL samples with various concentrations (10, 20, 40, 100, and 150 μg/mL), Triton X-100 (1 %) as a positive control, and PBS as a negative control were added. All samples were stored in a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37°C for 4 h. After 4 h, samples were centrifuged, and 100-μL supernatant was changed into a 96-well plate. The hemolysis index was measured at 540 nm by an ELISA reader. The percentages of hemolysis of samples were calculated as follows:
Where A comprises the OD (Optical Density) values of the test sample, positive control Triton X-100 (1 %), and negative control (PBS).

Human cervical cancer cell lines (HeLa) were obtained from ATCC and cultured in DMEM with the addition of 10 % FBS that contained 1 % penicillin–streptomycin in a T-75 culture flask at 37°C in an incubator with 5 % CO2 condition.

The cytotoxicity effects of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs were investigated under HeLa cells using the MTT method. The cells’ densities (1 × 105 cells/well) were seeded into 96-well plates in 100 μL of complete DMEM for 24 h. The culture medium was replaced with a new medium consisting of a series of concentrations (0.1–200 μg/mL) of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNP and incubated for 24 h. Thereafter, the medium was cleaned, and 100 μL of MTT assay solution with the concentration of 5 mg/mL was attached to per well plate and incubated for 4 h. In each well plate, the formazan crystals were dissolved at 100 μL of DMSO. The OD was measured using a microplate reader (ELx-800, BIOTEK instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) at 570 nm.

Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM): HeLa cells were seeded in a 35 mm microscopy dish at a density of 1 × 105 for 24 h. The medium was cleaned and replaced with a fresh serum-free medium consisting of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNP with different incubation times (3, 12, and 24 h). Following that, HeLa cells were mildly washed at least three times with PBS and tied with 4 % paraformaldehyde about 30 min at 25 °C. Then, the nuclei cells were stained by DAPI for 30 min. The fluorescence of cells was observed using a confocal microscopy (iRiS™ Digital Cell Imaging System).

Flow cytometry: HeLa cells were seeded in six-well plates at a density of 2 × 106 cells/well and incubated for 24 h at 37°C. Next, CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs were dissolved in fresh free-serum medium, attached to well plates, and cultured with various co-culture times for 1, 3, 6, 12, and 24 h. Then, the cells were treated by trypsin, resuspended with cold PBS (0.5 mL), and subjected to a flow cytometer (FACSAriaTM III; BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA, USA) to confirm the fluorescence signals of cells. The flow cytometry data were determined via FlowJo software.

Cyclin Dependent Kinase 2 (CDK2) protein was chosen as a target protein. β-CD and AZD5438 were used as target ligands and control ligands, respectively. CDK2 protein structure data (PDB ID: 6GUE) was retrieved from the RCSB protein database as a PDB file. Using Discovery Studio software, the unnecessary molecules from the PDB structure file were removed. β-CD SMILE structure was retrieved from the PubChem database (CID: 444041). β-CD 3D structure was created using Avogadro software with a source from SMILE structure. Protein–ligand molecular docking was completed using PyRx software. The results obtained from molecular docking were further analyzed using PyMOL and Discovery Studio software, including the binding affinity, 2D and 3D visualization, and amino acid residue.
The DLS, zeta potential, kinetic stability, and in vitro analysis (cell experiment) were repeated at least three times with the findings presented as the mean ± standard deviation.

Our strategy highlights the development of a CNP via a green synthesis of high-value-added peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells coated with a protective layer of β-CD, which can be utilized as bioimaging probes ( Scheme 1 ). CNPs were synthesized using a pyrolysis process at 250 °C and then directly immersed with β-CD to provide a protective layer of β-CD-coated CNPs. The surface state and composition of the CNPs were determined by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1 a, three peaks at 295, 400, and 534 eV were observed in the XPS spectra, which indicates the presence of C, N, and O elements in the CNPs. Next, we looked at the XRD patterns of the CNPs and revealed a broad peak (002) centered at approximately 22.5° with a small intensity ( Fig. 1 b). These results are due to the highly disordered carbon atoms, which are called an amorphous structure, and confirmed the graphene structure, suggesting that carbon materials are present in our peanut shells. Meanwhile, the representative peak in the XRD pattern of β-CD-coated CNPs showed no remarkable difference when compared with pristine β-CD, and the absence of a peak of approximately 22.5° suggests the existence of a carbon peak in the β-CD-coated CNPs, which is also conforming to an earlier investigation (Yang et al., 2014). The above results suggest that the obtained particles are N-doped nanoscaled carbonaceous material with various function groups comprised of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface.

Additionally, to understand optical properties as potential applications in bioimaging, we observed CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs, which were measured via UV-Vis, PL spectroscopy, and simple digital photographs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs show a border absorbance wavelength peak at 290 and 350 nm, which indicates that the sample can absorb the light intensity. The absorbance of β-CD-coated CNPs was slightly greater than that of CNPs, which indicates that β-CD-coated CNPs have superior stability in light-intensity absorption when compared with pristine CNPs. Additionally, the absorption spectra demonstrated a wide absorption band without an obvious characteristic peak, possibly because of the relatively broad size distribution of CNPs (Zhou et al., 2012). Next, the β-CD-coated CNPs showed a robust light-green fluorescence with a maximal emission peak at 440 nm and the intensity of fluorescence reached 5300 when excited at 350 nm, whereas the CNPs showed a fluorescence intensity of only 4500 ( Fig. 2 b). To prove the fluorescence properties, we also performed with visible and broadband long-wavelength lamp illumination. As shown in Fig. 2 c and d, the strong green fluorescence of β-CD-coated CNPs was clearly observed under the UV lamp in comparison with pristine CNPs. These fluorescence behaviors are different from those of other peanut skin-derived CNPs, which exhibit a blue fluorescence, probably owing to the difference in size and surface groups (Xue et al., 2016). Additionally, these results suggested that the protective layer of β-CD on the surface of CNPs could enhance the fluorescence by forming a stable structure within an aqueous solution. Overall, these findings demonstrate that the presence of a protective layer of β-CD within peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells CNPs can potentially be employed for bioimaging applications due to the enhanced and maintained stability of their fluorescence behaviors.

Moreover, the hydrodynamic diameter and structural morphology of the protective layer β-CD within CNPs were analyzed via DLS and SEM. As shown in Fig. 3 a, pristine CNPs at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL had a mean hydrodynamic diameter of 136 ± 3.35 nm. Conversely, the average hydrodynamic diameter of β-CD-coated CNPs was slightly higher under a concentration (0.1 mg/mL) was 180 ± 2.74 nm, which indicates that the β-CD effectively envelops the CNPs, which leads to an increase in its particle size compared to pristine CNPs ( Fig. 3 b). To validate the findings acquired by DLS, we analyzed the morphological structure of CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs via SEM. As presented in Fig. 3 c and d, the outer surface of the β-CD-coated CNPs is smooth, and the hydrodynamic diameter was slightly increased, whereas pristine CNPs have a rough surface. These results suggest that β-CD effectively covers the CNPs maintained in nanosized. In general, the β-CD-coated CNPs with nanosized (Wu et al., 2019) and surface properties (Chithrani and Chan, 2007) can potentially easily internalize into the cancer.

The colloidal stability of the nanocarrier was significantly influenced by the physiological normal environment (Mishra et al., 2010). In this study, the colloidal stability of the nanoparticles was determined via time-dependent fluorescence intensity variations in DMEM enriched with 10 % FBS and an antihemolytic study using MRBCs. As mention in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of the β-CD-coated CNPs solution remains constant over 24 h, which indicates that the β-CD-coated CNPs show a high level of structural stability in aqueous solution owing to the protected layer of β-CD. By contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs gradually decreased over 24 h, which implies that CNPs have an unstable nanostructure in an aqueous solution, which leads to decreasing fluorescence behavior ( Fig. 4 b). In the other analysis ( Fig. 4 c), the mean hydrodynamic diameter of pristine CNPs increased progressively from 136 to 360 nm after 24 h in a serum-rich medium. These results suggest that the CNPs were unable to maintain the nanostructure condition, which subsequently led to the formation of aggregated particles in aqueous solutions. Interestingly, the mean hydrodynamic diameter of β-CD-coated CNPs remained nearly unaltered after 24 h, which suggests that the nanostructure of the β-CD-coated CNPs was highly stable under typical physiological conditions (pH; 7.4), which was likely due to the protective layer of β-CD that enhanced the stability of the CNPs. This observation indicates the substantial stability of the steric stabilization provided by the β-CD fragment, which provides an raise it in stability, is mostly due to the improvement of the hydrophilicity of the CNP after the modification with β-CD (Karimi and Namazi, 2021). To provide more detailed stability evidence, we carried out an investigation into in vitro antihemolytic assay, which is a critical parameter in biological applications. As depicted in Fig. 4 d, the hemolytic assay of MRBCs showed that β-CD-coated CNPs induced only 3.46 % hemolysis at the highest concentration of 200 μg/mL. This suggests that β-CD-coated CNPs possess high blood compatibility and exhibit acceptable hemolytic toxicity, with levels below the 5 % threshold previously reported (Yu et al., 2020, Singh et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the CNPs showed a hemolytic activity of 28.72 % at the higher concentration, which is unacceptable for further study. Thus, the protective layer β-CD within peanut shells possesses excellent blood safety with MRBCs and potentially to evaluated in in vitro studies for bioimaging applications.

The quantitative cytotoxicity of pristine CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs was evaluated using MTT assays on HeLa cancer cell lines. As shown in Fig. 5 a, CNPs exhibited negligible toxicity toward HeLa cells across all concentrations (1–200 μg/mL) after 24 h of incubation, which indicates that the CNPs show exceptional biocompatibility. Surprisingly, the cell viability of HeLa cells demonstrated remarkable cytotoxic effects, with an IC50 value of 69.7 μg/mL. These suggest that β-CD-coated CNPs exhibit cytotoxicity toward cancer cell lines because of their interaction with the surface of cancer cells, leading to impaired drainage and subsequent toxicity within the cells (Nalepa et al., 2020, Riley and Narayan, 2021). Predominantly, these results obtained different toxicity from previous studies, which explained that peanut shells are very biocompatible with HepG2 cells (Xue et al., 2016); these findings provide insight that CNPs have high toxicity under HeLa cells after covert with β-CD probably due to the different types of peanut shells and solubility properties in aqueous solutions. Overall, these findings clearly reveal that β-CD-coated CNPs exhibit remarkable cytotoxic activity against cancer cell lines, potentially enhancing intracellular uptake and providing strong fluorescence activity within the cancer cells. To gain further insight into the mechanisms of intracellular uptake and cellular morphology as potential bioimaging applications of nanoparticles, we performed observations via CLSM. CNPs and β-CD-coated CNPs were incubated with cells for various co-culture durations (3, 12, or 24 h), after which the cells were stained with DAPI. The results revealed intense green fluorescence signals from the nanoparticles, and cell internalization was subsequently observed using CLSM. As shown in Fig. 5 b, HeLa cells exhibited weak green fluorescence intensity after 24 h of co-culture with CNPs. By contrast, β-CD-coated CNPs presented a notable green fluorescence after 3 h of co-culture, diffusely distributed throughout the cytoplasm. Then, a strong fluorescence signal was gradually detected within the nuclei of HeLa cells incubated with β-CD-coated CNPs for 24 h. However, the underlying mechanism of β-CD-coated CNPs remains unclear, and further investigations are necessary to elucidate these findings. To further confirm the intracellular uptake, quantitative flow cytometry analysis was performed to provide additional insights into the extent of nanoparticle internalization by the cells with fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. 5 c, the nanoparticles were internalized by HeLa cells, thus resulting in a minimal increase in intracellular fluorescence intensity after 24 h of incubation. By contrast, β-CD-coated CNPs were rapidly internalized, with a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity observed as the incubation time with HeLa cells was extended ( Fig. 5 d). These results indicate that β-CD-coated CNPs can rapidly internalize into HeLa cells with a high degree of fluorescence intensity. Overall, our evaluation of this newly developed system clearly demonstrates that peanut shells can be utilized to synthesize CNPs, subsequently enhancing intracellular stable fluorescence with a β-CD protective layer, which has provided for bioimaging applications for tracking cancer cells and detecting cellular events, including mitosis and cell division.

Furthermore, to gain a deeper understanding of the mechanism by which nanoparticles are internalized into cancer cells, molecular docking analysis was conducted to assess the interaction between the nanoparticle compounds and CDK-2. CDK-2 is a protein that plays a crucial role in the cell cycle, particularly during the G1/S and S phases, and is identified in the nucleus of HeLa cells (Bačević et al., 2017; Jaumot et al., 1994; Lin et al., 2000). The expression vectors for Cyclin E/CDK2 have been demonstrated to promote transient Human Papilloma Virus (HPV) replication in these cells, thereby establishing CDK2 as a pivotal target for inhibiting cancer cell growth. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, β-CD-coated CNPs exhibited notable activity against CDK-2 by effectively interacting with and inhibiting these proteins, highlighting their potential therapeutic applications. Importantly, β-CD-coated CNPs showed remarkable interactions with CDK-2 proteins via the formation of hydrogen bonds or hydrophobic interactions ( Fig. 6 c and d). In comparison with the control ligand, β-CD-coated CNPs demonstrated the ability to interact with amino acid residues LEU83 and PHE80, indicating that there is an interaction with AZD5438 ligand. This similarity in binding behavior shows the potential of β-CD-coated CNPs as a viable alternative for targeting CDK-2 proteins, thereby enhancing its applicability in bioimaging activity as well as toxicity strategies toward cancer cells. As listed in Table S1, the binding affinity of the β-CD-coated CNPs and CDK-2 complex are higher than that of the AZD5438 and CDK-2 complex. It is well-established that a lower binding affinity indicates more stable ligand–protein interaction leading to greater efficacy of the ligand against the target protein. Therefore, the higher binding affinity of the β-CD and CDK-2 complexes suggests a comparatively reduced activity of β-CD against CDK-2 (Shallangwa and Adeniji, 2021). These results might be due to the β-CD acting mainly as a protective layer of CNPs and delivering drugs or compounds to their targets. Thus, future studies could focus on exploring other types of nanoparticles with their potential to protect layers and effectively deliver CDK-2 inhibitor drugs, which leads to better ways of enhancing the strength and effectiveness of CDK-2 inhibition treatments.

In summary, we have successfully developed a β-CD protective layer within peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells, which serves as a highly stable fluorescent probe for bioimaging applications. The resulting synthesized material exhibited a protective β-CD layer within the peanut shells, having a nanosized structure and a smooth surface morphology. Predominantly, β-CD-coated CNPs demonstrated excellent photoluminescence and optical absorption properties for a long period. Furthermore, these nanoparticles have stable nanostructure under a serum-rich medium and high biocompatibility with a low percentage of hemolytic activity MRBCs, which potentially enhances stable fluorescence under an aqueous solution. In vitro cytotoxicity studies clearly showed that β-CD-coated CNPs strongly inhibited the activity toward HeLa, when compared with pristine CNPs. Predominantly, intracellular analysis shows that β-CD-coated CNPs have rapid intracellular uptake and stable fluorescence under HeLa cells. An in silico study revealed that β-CD-coated CNPs could interact and efficacy of targeting CDK-2 in G1/S and S phases, which plays an important role in terms of inhibiting the growth of cancer cells. Overall, the present study emphasizes the advancements in the green synthesis of CNPs from peanut (Arachis hypogaea L.) shells, which provide stable fluorescence with a β-CD protective layer potentially as tracking cancer cells and detecting cellular events.

Fasih Bintang Ilhami: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sapti Puspitarini: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Astrid Rahmawati: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation. Noor Rohmah Mayasari: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Hepy Herliniati: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author(s) declare(s) that there is no conflict of interest regarding the publication of this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.plana.2025.100135.
